title: Built-in RBAC Roles - Azure IoT Operations
display_title: Use built-in RBAC roles for Azure IoT Operations access control
description: Learn about the built-in RBAC roles for Azure IoT Operations and how to use them to control access to resources.
summary: Azure IoT Operations (AIO) offers two built-in roles designed to simplify and secure access management for AIO resources: Azure IoT Operations Administrator and Azure IoT Operations Onboarding. If your scenario requires more granular access, you cancreate a custom RBAC role. Important The built-in roles for AIO streamline access management for AIO resources, but don't automatically grant permissions for all required Azure dependencies. AIO relies on several Azure services, such as Azure Key Vaul
classification_reason: Defines specific built-in RBAC role names and their scope, which is product-specific security configuration.

summary: Azure IoT Operations uses TLS to encrypt communication between all components. This article describes how to manage certificates for internal and external communications, and how to bring your own certificate authority (CA) issuer for internal communications in a production deployment.

title: Manage secrets - Azure IoT Operations
display_title: Manage Azure IoT Operations secrets with Key Vault and Kubernetes
description: Create, update, and manage secrets that are required to give your Arc-enabled Kubernetes cluster access to Azure resources.
summary: Azure IoT Operations uses Azure Key Vault as the managed vault solution on the cloud, and usesAzure Key Vault Secret Store extension for Kubernetesto sync the secrets down from the cloud and store them on the edge as Kubernetes secrets. Edge resources like connectors and dataflows can then use these secrets for authentication when connecting to external systems.
classification_reason: Describes how Azure IoT Operations uses Key Vault and the Secret Store extension to sync secrets to edge as Kubernetes secrets, a product-specific security pattern.

title: Validate images - Azure IoT Operations
display_title: Validate Azure IoT Operations container and Helm images
description: Validate that Azure IoT Operations docker and helm images are legitimate.
summary: Azure IoT Operations signs its docker and helm images to allow users to verify the integrity and origin of the images they use. Signing utilizes a public/private key pair to prove that Microsoft built a container image by creating a digital signature and adding it to the image. This article provides the steps to verify that an image was signed by Microsoft. Download Notation. Download the Microsoft signing public certificate:https://www.microsoft.com/pkiops/certs/Microsoft%20Supply%20Chain%20RSA
classification_reason: Provides concrete steps and URLs for verifying signed images, a product-specific supply-chain security configuration.
